setup.materials: large tub of water, cork, marble, plastic spoon, metal key, apple
setup.question: Which objects sink and which ones float?

root.narration: There is a big tub of water on the table, and your teacher gives you five objects: a cork, a marble, a plastic spoon, a metal key, and an apple. She asks, 'Which ones will float and which will sink?' How do you find the answer?

choices.text: Gently place each object in the water one at a time. Write down which ones float and which ones sink.
Say that heavy things sink and light things float, without testing.
Throw all five objects into the water at the same time to see what happens.

outcome.headline: Excellent! You Tested and Recorded!
outcome.explanation: By testing one at a time, you could clearly see what happened to each object. The cork, plastic spoon, and apple floated! The marble and metal key sank. You might be surprised the apple floated -- it is big but it has tiny air pockets inside that help it float. Writing down your results is what real scientists do!
outcome.whatToRemember: Test objects one at a time and write down what happens. Size does not tell you if something floats -- it depends on the material.

keyTerms.definition: When an object stays on top of the water instead of falling to the bottom.
When an object falls to the bottom of the water because it is denser than the water.

outcome.headline: Good Thinking, But Not Quite Right!
outcome.explanation: Heavy things do often sink, but not always! A big apple is heavier than a tiny marble, but the apple floats and the marble sinks. It is not just about weight -- it is about what the object is made of. An apple has air inside it. A marble is solid glass. Testing would have shown you this surprise!
outcome.whatToRemember: Whether something sinks or floats is not just about weight. The material matters too! Always test to find out.

outcome.headline: Too Fast! You Could Not Tell What Happened!
outcome.explanation: When you throw everything in at once, it is hard to see which object floated and which one sank. Objects bump into each other and make a big splash. Scientists test one thing at a time so they can see exactly what happens. Try again, but this time put in one object, watch it, then try the next one.
outcome.whatToRemember: Test one object at a time so you can clearly see what happens to each one.
